data.title: Endava Is Maintained at Positive by Susquehanna
Canada Consumer Might Still Have Momentum After Strong December — Market Talk
data.description: Canada's retail data for December indicate consumers flocked to stores that month to capitalize on a temporary sales-tax exemption. Retail receipts grew 2.5% from the prior month, or well above expectations. Tony Stillo, economist at Oxford Economics, says the data suggests consumers delayed expenditures in November until the sales-tax holiday kicked in. Momentum didn't carry over to January, with Statistics Canada estimating retail sales fell 0.4% after a strong December. Stillo says spending could still hold up, as the sales-tax holiday only ended a week ago--although he's unsure how much President Trump's threat of hefty tariffs may cause households to curtail spending, especially for big-ticket items. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

data.title: US 10 Year Treasury Bond Note Yield at 4.46percent
data.description:
The yield on the 10-year US Treasury note fell toward 4.45% on Friday, the lowest in two weeks, as pessimistic economic data magnified the impact of favorable developments in the US bond market.

Fresh data showed that the US services sector unexpectedly contracted in February as concerns about lower government spending drove clients to halt new orders, marking a sharp swing for the sector that had been resilient for two years.

The data favored bets that the Fed is due to lower interest rates this year, with only 15% of the market positioned for no cuts by December.

Meanwhile, the Treasury signaled it will not increase the share of longer term securities in the near future, limiting the supply of securities in the longer end of the curve.

This compounded support for 10-year notes after FOMC minutes showed that the Fed thinks it may be appropriate to pause asset selling until the resolution of debt ceiling dynamics, setting the stage for a potential end to quantitative tightening.

data.title: Covalon Swings to Profit On Higher Revenue in Q1
data.description:
Covalon Technologies , an advanced medical technologies company, on Friday reported a swing to profit and an increase in revenue for the first quarter of fiscal 2025 due to strong demand in its U.S. medical consumables business.

The company reported net income of $1.2 million for the three months ended Dec. 31, compared with a year-ago net loss figure of $851,543. Earnings per share basic and diluted for the quarter was $0.04, versus loss per share of $0.03 for both, a year earlier.

Total revenue increased to near $8.2 million compared to near $4.7 million in the same period of the prior year. Overall revenue grew 75%, including 73% growth in the US Medical Consumables business, which "remains our primary focus".

However, Covalon Chief Executive Brent Ashton said the company experienced a "slight sequential slowdown" in its US Collagen business due to some normalization in channel inventory. "We expect this slowdown to reverse in the second half of fiscal 2025, supported by ongoing conversations and forecasts received from our customers."

Shares were last seen down more than 5%, amid losses for the broader market.

data.title: MORE US CONSUMER SENTIMENT: INFLATION EXPECTATIONS UP IN FEB. FOR DEMOCRATS AND INDEPENDENTS, DOWN FOR REPUBLICANS
Home Sales Sagged in January as Prices Continue to Rise — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Shaina Mishkin

Home buyers and sellers had a tough winter, new data confirm. Mortgage applications in the coming weeks will tell if the freeze carries into the spring.

Previously owned homes were sold at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 4.08 million in January, according to National Association of Realtors data published Friday. Sales fell 4.9% from the previous month to the slowest pace since October. Consensus had expected a shallower drop, to a roughly 4.1 million pace, according to FactSet. Sales were 2% better than in January 2024, but remain relatively low historically.

Sales dragged as housing costs remained high. The median home sold for $396,900 — up 4.8% from the January prior. Costs picked up most in the northeast, west, and midwest: the median sale price in these regions rose 9.5%, 7.4%, and 7.2%, respectively. Gains were slowest in the south, which registered a 3.5% increase.

"Mortgage rates have refused to budge for several months despite multiple rounds of short-term interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve, " Lawrence Yun, the trade group's chief economist, said in a statement. "When combined with elevated home prices, housing affordability remains a major challenge."

The measure is the main way analysts, economists, and industry professionals assess historic trends in the national housing resale market, which comprises a much bigger share of all sales than new construction. Since it can take a month or two for home sales to close, the figure reflects completed sales that went under contract a month or two prior.

That time gap feels particularly wide as the industry looks for signs of strength ahead of the spring.

Mortgage rates have steadied in recent weeks, though they remain at levels near 7%, according to Freddie Mac. The average 30-year mortgage rate this week was 6.85%. "This stability continues to bode well for potential buyers and sellers as we approach the spring homebuying season, " Sam Khater, Freddie Mac's chief economist, said in a statement.

Some real estate agents have reported early signs of buyer demand ahead of the spring season — but whether those signs translate into a significant pickup in sales has yet to be seen. Weekly applications for home purchase loans, a leading indicator of buyer demand published by the Mortgage Bankers Association, dropped a seasonally adjusted 6% the week of Feb. 14, from the week prior. The index remains at a level well below the seasonally adjusted long-term average.

Coming trends for the spring will be reflected first in mortgage application data before trickling down into closed home sales measures. Investors should keep their eyes on the trade group's weekly index, which is released on Wednesday mornings, to monitor the start of the season.

Write to Shaina Mishkin at shaina.mishkin@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Consumer sentiment sours further in late February
data.description:
The second of two readings of consumer sentiment in February slipped to 64.7 from 67.8 earlier in the month. Sentiment has fallen nearly 10% from January. It's the lowest since November 2023.

Long-run inflation expectations moved higher, the University of Michigan reported.

This is breaking news. Check back for updates.BX:TMUBMUSD10Y SPX

-Greg Robb

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Bandwidth Price Target Maintained With a $36.00/Share by Citizens Capital Markets
Rivian Automotive's 2025 Guidance 'Very Hittable' Amid 'Murky' Macro Backdrop, Wedbush Says
data.description:
Rivian Automotive's 2025 guidance is "very hittable" as it faces a "murky" macro backdrop, Wedbush Securities said in a note to clients Friday.

"RIVN provided relatively conservative FY25 guidance as the company battles a myriad of macro headwinds while looking to balance the ramp of its vehicle lines to generate stable revenue growth while remaining prudent on spend to drive bottom-line expansion," Wedbush analysts said.

The analysts said the company's delivery guidance of 46,000 to 51,000 vehicles for 2025 implies a year-over-year negative growth in deliveries.

"We continue to remain confident in the long-term RIVN vision that is in the midst of massive transformation while looking to optimize its R1 production while preparing to ramp its R2 and midsize platform supply chains," the analysts said.

Wedbush maintained Rivian's outperform rating and $20 price target.

Rivian shares were down nearly 4% in recent Friday trading.

data.title: World Kinect Is Maintained at Hold by Stifel
Super Micro Stock Rises. Why Next Week Will Be Key for SMCI. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By George Glover

Super Micro Computer Inc. stock was rising Friday, signaling that investors are still bullish on the server maker ahead of a crucial week.

Shares, which trade under the ticker SMCI, climbed 1.1% to $59.90 in premarket trading. Futures tracking the benchmark S&P 500 were flat.

The stock has jumped 10 of the past 12 days, powered higher by future revenue guidance that came in well above analysts' expectations. That surge has helped SMCI to pass software platform provider Palantir Technologies to become the best-performing S&P 500 stock of 2025.

Next week could make or break the rally. The Nasdaq has given Super Micro until Tuesday to file its accounts for the year ended June 30 and first-quarter earnings for the three months ended Sept. 30 — otherwise, it faces the risk of being delisted. The company said in last week's business update that it "believes it will make such filings" before the Nasdaq's deadline.

Write to George Glover at george.glover@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Solaris Energy Shares Climb on Generation Capacity Expansion
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Shares of Solaris Energy gained after it said it was expanding its generating capacity in 2025.

The stock rose 23% to $36.67 in premarket trading Friday, after touching a 52-week high of $37.44. The share price has increased nearly five-fold over the past year.

The solar energy company said it expects quarterly revenue generating capacity to increase to 360 megawatts in the first quarter of 2025 and 420 megawatts by the midpoint of this year, up from 260 megawatts in the fourth quarter. It expects revenue to grow sequentially with the capacity expansion, it said.

Solaris Chief Executive Bill Zartler said there is more demand for behind-the-meter projects, which generally refers to solar panels that give energy directly to residential and business customers without passing through a meter. Solaris has secured about 700 megawatts of new power-generation capacity to approximately double its operated fleet over the next two years, Zartler said.

Solaris posted a fourth-quarter profit of $5.8 million, or 19 cents a share, compared with $4.1 million, or 14 cents a share, the previous year.

Revenue doubled to $96.3 million. Analysts polled by FactSet were expecting $92.7 million.

Stripping out certain one-time items, adjusted per-share earnings were 12 cents, in line with Wall Street's expectations.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: French 10-Year Bond Yield Falls at End of Week
data.description:
The yield on France’s 10-year government bond dropped to 3.2% after weaker-than-expected PMI data reinforced expectations of deeper interest rate cuts by the European Central Bank this year.

However, the yield remained on track for its biggest weekly gain since January, driven by concerns over increased borrowing to fund higher defense spending following talks between U.S. President Trump and Russian President Putin on ending the Ukraine war.

February’s PMI data showed the steepest contraction in France’s private sector in a year, with the index plunging to 44.5, well below market forecasts of 48.0.

Meanwhile, Eurozone business growth remained sluggish, supported mainly by modest expansions in Germany and other European economies.

Investors are also closely watching Germany’s federal election on Sunday, as Europe’s largest economy grapples with stagnation.

data.title: UnitedHealth's Sell-Off On DOJ Probe Report Is Buying Opportunity, Mizuho Says — Market Talk
data.description: UnitedHealth Group is sharply lower after WSJ reports that the Department of Justice is investigating the company's Medicare billing practices. Mizuho analysts say in a report that while the investigation is new, the issues brought into question aren't. They don't believe there will be an incremental negative impact to earnings from the probe. "We would be buyers of UNH on the weakness," the analysts say. UnitedHealth sinks 9%. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

data.title: U.S. Activity Closes in on Stalling as Tariff Threats Hurt Sentiment
data.description:
By Ed Frankl

U.S. economic activity came close to stagnating this month, as uncertainty about government policies related to spending cuts and tariffs adversely affect business sentiment, according to a monthly survey.

The S&P Global Flash U.S. Composite PMI--which gauges activity in the manufacturing and services sectors--sank to 50.4 in February from 52.7 in January, the weakest level in 17 months, fresh purchasing managers' index surveys said Friday. The reading above 50 suggests overall activity continued to expand.

"The upbeat mood seen among U.S. businesses at the start of the year has evaporated, replaced with a darkening picture of heightened uncertainty, stalling business activity and rising prices," S&P Global Market Intelligence Chief Business Economist Chris Williamson said.

A renewed fall in services output offset faster manufacturing growth, though that latter was in part linked to front-running of tariffs, hinting at only a temporary boost, S&P said.

The overall result dragged optimism from multiyear highs at the turn of the year to one of the gloomiest readings since the pandemic, Williamson continued.

Input cost pressures meanwhile spiked higher, a concern for the Federal Reserve that expressed little appetite for rate cuts in the near term, according to minutes of the January policy meeting published this week.

Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com

data.title: January Home Sales Fall 4.9%, Extending Slump in Housing Market — WSJ
data.description:
By Nicole Friedman

Sales of existing homes fell in January, starting the year on a down note after two consecutive years of extremely sluggish sales.

Home prices and mortgage rates, which were a drag on home sales for most of 2024, stayed elevated last month and put off many buyers. Some who are shopping for their first home have been priced out of the market, and many homeowners have decided to stay put rather than move and give up a low mortgage rate.

U.S. existing-home sales fell 4.9% in January from the prior month to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 4.08 million, the National Association of Realtors said Friday. Economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal had estimated a monthly decrease of 2.6%.

In 2024, home sales fell to the lowest level since 1995 for the second straight year.

Real-estate agents are hoping that home-sales activity will pick up in the spring, which is typically the busiest time of year for home sales. Inventory is rising because some sellers need to move due to life changes and can't keep waiting for affordability to improve.

Yet that might not be enough to get the housing market going again. Economists largely expect mortgage rates, which topped 7% last month and remain near there, to stay around current levels in the coming months. Rising home insurance and property tax costs are also adding to the cost of homeownership.

"In the month of January, this loosening of inventory did not bring additional buyers because of the higher mortgage rates," said Lawrence Yun, NAR's chief economist. "Consumers are essentially tapped out."

Home sales are rising on an annual basis. January sales were up 2% from a year earlier, the fourth consecutive month of year-over-year increases.

Write to Nicole Friedman at nicole.friedman@wsj.com

data.title: Walmart's Fiscal Q4 Results 'Solid' Despite Flipkart Sale Timing Shift, Deutsche Bank Says
data.description:
Walmart reported "solid" fiscal Q4 results despite the change in schedule of Flipkart's Big Billion Days sale as China and Canada units' performance remained strong, Deutsche Bank said in a note Friday.

The company's Q4 same store sales and total sales in China jumped 23.1% and 27.7%, respectively, while Canada posted 5.8% same store sales growth and 5.5% increase in net sales, according to the note.

"SSS, by our calculation, accelerated to +8.3% vs. +6.1% in Q3, driven by successful festive events across markets and robust general merchandise growth," Deutsche Bank said.

The retail giant's fiscal 2025 guidance had "some noise," with a bigger-than-anticipated 80 basis points profitability growth drag from Vizio, the note said, adding that Walmart's initial 2025 earnings before interest and taxes growth range of 5% to 7% is "solid."

Deutsche Bank lowered Walmart's price target to $113 from $115, and maintained a buy rating on the stock.

data.title: US Year-Ahead Inflation Expectations at Over 1-Year High
data.description:
The year-ahead inflation expectations in the United States, as compiled by the University of Michigan, reached an over one-year high of 4.3% in February 2025, up sharply from 3.3% in January, according to final figures.

In the meantime, the five-year outlook was revised higher to 3.5% in February, compared to a preliminary reading of 3.3% and above 3.2% in January.

data.title: Michigan Consumer Sentiment Revised Sharply Lower
data.description:
The University of Michigan consumer sentiment for the US was revised sharply lower to 64.7 in February 2025 from a preliminary of 67.8, reaching the lowest level since November 2023.

The gauge for current economic conditions was also revised lower to 65.7 from 68.7 and the expectations subindex was revised down to 64 from 67.3.

The decrease in sentiment was unanimous across groups by age, income, and wealth.

The drop was led by a 19% plunge in buying conditions for durables, in large part due to fears that tariff-induced price increases are imminent.

Expectations for personal finances and the short-run economic outlook both declined almost 10%, while the long-run economic outlook fell back about 6% to its lowest reading since November 2023.

Meanwhile, inflation expectations for the year-ahead soared to 4.3%, the highest since November 2023.

The five-year outlook increased to 3.5% from a preliminary of 3.3% and above 3.2% in January, the largest mom increase seen since May 2021.

data.title: US Inventory Of Unsold US Homes At 3.5 Months Supply
The Man Who Bought Fire Island — WSJ
data.description:
By Candace Taylor | Photography by Ryan Lowry for WSJ. Magazine

Tristan Schukraft almost didn't go to Fire Island.

A California native, the serial tech entrepreneur was in New York City on a business trip about 12 years ago when friends invited him to part of the island known as the Pines, a famous gay vacation destination on a sliver of sand off Long Island.

Schukraft was skeptical. "My friend hit me up and said, 'You should come to Fire Island — you can sleep on the sofa,' " he recalls. "I was like, 'That's not really my thing.' " The jet-setting tech founder, now 45, prefers luxury hotels to the cramped "share houses" where many Fire Island vacationers stay. "You're in this house with a bunch of people you don't know — it's so awkward to me."

But he went, borrowing cash for the 30-minute ferry ride — "No one told me you need cash for the ferry!" — and ended up loving the wooded island, where cars are prohibited but deer and scantily clad men roam.

Still, Schukraft, based in Los Angeles and then Puerto Rico, made only one other short day trip to the Pines over the next 10 years. So in May 2024, he raised eyebrows by announcing a deal to pay about $17 million for the vast majority of the Pines commercial district, including a hotel, restaurant and nightclub and the iconic Blue Whale bar, where the Pines' famous "tea dance" party was created for gay singles in the 1960s.

Schukraft sees himself as a cultural preservationist, and the Pines is one part of an ambitious project to protect gay history and restore glory to landmark gay institutions. In April 2024, he paid $45 million for the Abbey Food & Bar, the inspiration for Chappell Roan's song "Pink Pony Club" and an icon of gay life in West Hollywood that had in recent years been beset by a series of complaints that patrons were drugged there. The problem is widespread and not unique to the Abbey, Schukraft says; the bar provides free test strips to customers and does staff trainings focused on the issue.

Schukraft is also reopening the shuttered nightclub Circo in Puerto Rico, and he bought the D.S. Tequila Co. nightclub in Chicago's Boystown neighborhood.

Some of these efforts have been met with skepticism, but none more than Schukraft's acquisition on Fire Island. The Pines — and the nearby community of Cherry Grove — are some of the gay community's most sacred spaces. For decades, the island's secluded location allowed gay people a temporary escape from their otherwise closeted lives. Its politically engaged, vocal residents are fiercely protective of the area's history and traditions, such as the tea dance and the annual Fourth of July drag queen "Invasion."

"We have a very opinionated, critical community who all have something to say," says Bobby Bonanno, founder of the Fire Island Pines Historical Society. Former commercial district owner Ian Reisner learned that the hard way in 2015, when he faced a boycott after he hosted a Manhattan event with same-sex-marriage opponent Ted Cruz.

Fire Island's status as a haven for the gay community is particularly crucial in the current political moment, Bonanno says. "You can be who you want, you can be with who you want — in these political times that becomes even more important."

Schukraft knows his plans are under a microscope. "I think we're going to bring a breath of fresh air into Fire Island — I think people are really going to like it," he says. After all, he has overcome adversity in business before. "I'm in sales," he says, "so no means yes."

Then again, Schukraft is not only a newcomer to the Pines, but has little experience running hotels or nightclubs. The founder of tech companies including the HIV telemedicine platform Mistr and the e-ticketing company ID90.com, Schukraft is now staging an audacious foray into the hospitality industry. On Fire Island, he will soon open an outpost of the first gay luxury hotel brand, Tryst Hotels, with other locations launching in Puerto Vallarta, Mexico, and San Juan, Puerto Rico. This series of rapid-fire acquisitions — focused on some of the most iconic institutions of gay life — have earned him the nickname "The CEO of Everything Gay."

On a recent afternoon at his new Tryst Puerto Vallarta hotel, a confident Schukraft looks the part in Hermès slip-ons, no-show socks and a crisp, pale-blue button-down. He wouldn't specify his net worth but says he's willing to spend big to ensure the precarious survival of beloved institutions like the Abbey and the Pines.

"Right now in the community, you have all these forefathers that are retiring," he says. "If somebody doesn't step in and take over, they're going to disappear."

Techno music thumps through the open-air restaurant at the Tryst Puerto Vallarta, where Schukraft is sampling new menu items. The hotel is in the last stages of construction and is scheduled to officially open this March, but the restaurant has already started hosting Drag Brunch on Sundays; attendees receive hot-pink fans with the Tryst logo on them.

Tanned and fit with sandy hair, Schukraft is describing his work-hard, play-hard lifestyle. "On Friday I'm exhausted from the workweek, but on Sunday I'm even more exhausted from partying all weekend," he says.

In some ways, he doesn't fit the mold of a nightlife impresario. "What is the definition of a crepe?" he asks the server who places a plate of chipotle chicken crepes on the table in front of him. When it comes to dining, Schukraft's tastes run mostly to calorie counting. "I'm a big vodka sugar-free Red Bull fan — that's my drink of choice," he says, tentatively sipping the hotel's blackberry martini, which is garnished with gold-coated blackberries. "But I'm trying to venture out."

He doesn't like gin or whiskey, and too many margaritas irritate his throat, but he does drink vodka with diet cranberry juice. "No bar ever serves diet cranberry — I don't get it," he says. "But it's something we're going to do here. That's one of the things about all my properties. I'm really trying to put my personal touch on them — so things I like, which hopefully other people like."

At the Tryst Puerto Vallarta, that means a palette of whites and neutrals with the occasional splash of pink, like the salmon-colored cushions on the cafe chairs. The new Tryst hotel in the Pines, slated to open in mid-May, will have a similar look, as will the Tryst San Juan, which will soon undergo renovation. Schukraft also hopes to launch the Tryst Ipanema in Brazil sometime in the next few years.

Schukraft grew up outside Los Angeles in a family of airline-industry employees — his father was a top executive at Northwest — and earned pocket money by returning luggage carts at the airport for 25 cents each. "We made like $30 and thought that was a lot of money," he recalls.

He spent much of his time at LAX, where at 17 he was discovered by Boss Model Management. He moved to Europe to pursue modeling, missing his last year of high school and taking his GED. "I don't know why my parents said yes," he says. Soon after that, he told them he was gay in a tearful scene in the family's garage. "My dad thought I was going to be held back in life career-wise. My mother thought I was going to get AIDS and die," Schukraft remembers. "My parents were very liberal, but it's different when it's your son."

Still, they were supportive, as were his two brothers and the rest of his extended family. "Everybody had to accept it or deal with my mother, " he says.

Modeling soon gave way to various business ventures, such as selling Razor scooters at local swap meets. In the early 2000s, Schukraft hit on a winning idea with ID90, a travel-booking website for airline personnel.

ID90 later evolved into a platform for e-ticketing — still new in the early 2000s. "Here I am, I'm this young kid trying to tell [airlines] how to run their business," Schukraft recalls. Because his technology would allow airlines to hire fewer employees, "I was not very well liked in the airline industry. People were like, 'Why do we need e-tickets? We have paper tickets.' It was so tough." When Hawaiian Airlines eventually became his first client, "I was on fumes," he says. "I've never been happier to fly to Hawaii." Other airlines started signing on, and the company soon had 111 employees around the world.

After 14 years of constant travel — "I know every airport code there is" — Schukraft wanted a break. He stepped back from ID90, although he and his parents still own a 30 percent stake in the company, and moved to West Hollywood. After a month, he was bored. So he ran an unsuccessful campaign for West Hollywood City Council and invested in the gay magazine Frontiers. His next big hit was Mistr, which launched shortly before Covid, becoming the largest telemedicine platform for HIV prevention and care. Today it serves half a million patients, Schukraft says, adding that he has also invested in real estate and other ventures.

His abrupt pivot into hotels and nightclubs is partly altruistic, Schukraft says. After moving to Puerto Rico with his partner, Melvin Alexander, during the pandemic, Schukraft discovered that Hurricane Maria — and Circo's closing — had decimated the island's gay nightlife. "One gay institution leaves, and then another one leaves, and before you know it, there's no gay community there," he says. To revive it, he bought Circo and the Tryst San Juan hotel. "My idea is, Build it, and they will come," he says.

The move also makes business sense, Schukraft says: Owning gay bars and hotels is a great way to promote Mistr. Free for users, Mistr makes its money through fees paid by nonprofits via a government program that subsidizes the cost of HIV prevention drugs; the company may soon face challenges to its business model as Congress considers changes to the program.

Schukraft also sees a void in the marketplace for uber-luxury gay hotels. While there are some hotel chains aimed at gay customers, Schukraft envisions a higher level of service. "There are gays that want the five-star experience where they can be true and authentic to themselves," he says. "Why do we have to settle for a four-star?" In the past, investors have shied away from the niche, arguing that there aren't enough wealthy gay customers. "I'm debunking the myth," he says, "that the gay market's not big enough."

Until recently, Schukraft and Alexander were part of a well-publicized throuple. On social media Schukraft posted images of the three posing in Speedos. But the trio broke up this past summer, and over the holidays Schukraft proposed to Alexander, with his family looking on, in what they believe to be the first gay marriage proposal at the South Pole. "He's the center of my world, why not the center of the world, right?" Schukraft says.

The glamorous lifestyle Schukraft documents on Instagram, along with his status as a Pines newcomer, has led to a certain amount of consternation among Fire Island residents, who fear his changes won't reflect the Pines' laid-back, barefoot vibe and storied history. The tea dance, for example, was created at a time when men weren't allowed to dance together in public; line dances such as the Hully Gully, with a sprinkling of women here and there, allowed them to sidestep that rule.

Schukraft "has no history there whatsoever," Bonanno says. "My concern is paying attention to the past and respecting what has come before. When people go to the Pines," he adds, "they're not looking for Manhattan skyscrapers and metal. They're looking for a beach aesthetic. You've got to honor that."

The Pines is also a notoriously difficult and expensive place to build. Materials must be delivered by boat, contractors can charge what they want, and the island is extremely seasonal. Virtually deserted between September and May, it leaves businesses a slim three-month window to make a profit. "It's a very punishing environment," says Andrew Kirtzman, another former owner of the Pines commercial district.

Schukraft is focused on the ways he can run Pines businesses better than in the past. For starters, he wants to rebrand the entire district. " 'Commercial district' is like 'public storage' to me," he says. "It does not sound sexy whatsoever. We're going to rename it 'the Waterfront.' "

Everyone acknowledges, meanwhile, that the buildings were badly in need of renovation when he bought them. The 1960s-era cinderblock hotel with shared bathrooms, nicknamed The Botel, "was an eyesore," Kirtzman says. It was condemned two years ago and hasn't opened since.

When renovations are completed, the 30-room hotel will have not only private bathrooms, but an elevator. Meanwhile, Schukraft is revamping the menu at the Canteen restaurant, banishing the "frozen cafeteria food" it had become known for.

"With very little effort we'll make more money this year," Schukraft says. "Just having a place to eat is going to have a huge impact."

Schukraft has long prided himself on his ability to move quickly. In all his businesses, he "just wants everything done yesterday," says his friend Karen Bauer. But that has already led to frustration in the Pines, a tiny village where "no one gets up before 11 a.m.," says Scott Bromley of Bromley Caldari Architects, a longtime Pines homeowner who is working with Schukraft on his plans for the commercial district. "Things don't just get done by snapping your fingers or if you have a lot of money. It's pedantic and a little slow-moving at times."

After making his Pines acquisition, for example, Schukraft had to wait months longer than expected for the liquor license to transfer over.

"I'm not sure it's going to all get built by this next season," says Bromley. "They have great ideas and they do a great job, but they just don't know how it really works on Fire Island."

Another open question is if Schukraft can make the Pines — and his other hospitality ventures — profitable, given the amount of money he is pouring into them. He declined to specify how much the Pines project will cost, but said he is bankrolling it himself and is spending $3 million on staff housing alone: He is revamping "the ranch," two small buildings where Pines workers previously lived in tiny rooms with bunk beds and shared bathrooms. Among his improvements: Wi-Fi and TVs in the rooms. "A lot of people think I'm crazy, it's not necessary," he says. "But if you treat your employees well, they work harder for you."

Still, Schukraft freely admits that he may never recoup his investment. "Serving the gay community is a passion project," he says. It's more about leaving a legacy than maximizing profits.

"Someone said to me, 'If you have to put in an extra $1 million to make this perfect, you will.' That is my personality," Schukraft says. "At the end of the day, my name is on it."

He takes a tiny bite of "Tryst Leches" cake, made from a friend's mother's recipe. "I recognize that you can't make everyone happy," he says. "When you try to make everybody happy, you make no one happy."

Write to Candace Taylor at candace.taylor@wsj.com

data.title: Palantir Stock Rises After 2-Day Slump. Where Wall Street Thinks It Goes Next. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Callum Keown

Friday looks set to be a crucial day for Palantir Technologies stock after its two-day 15% slump as investors assess whether the weakness is a blip, or something more sustained.

The shares are pointing 1% higher ahead of the open so the damage seems to have paused for now. It's still the third-best performer in the S&P 500 this year, climbing 40% so far in 2025.

Two things in particular spooked investors earlier this week — CEO Alex Karp's new plan to sell up to $1.2 billion worth of shares and reports that the Trump administration has warned the U.S. Department of Defense about budget cuts. The data analytics company has several military contracts for the use of its artificial intelligence technology.

Both of those things, in the context of Palantir's impressive rally and lofty valuation, have caused concern and raised questions over whether a period of prolonged weakness lies ahead for the stock.

While a third consecutive day of losses does not mean it's the start of a broader slump, investors would certainly hope to see the streak end and some stability return.

Wall Street thinks the stock has further to fall — analysts covering the shares have an average price target of $95.28, implying 10% downside to Thursday's closing price. Just 31% have Buy ratings on the stock, with 50% saying Hold and 19% recommending Sell.

The shares are expensive, trading at 190 times future earnings after jumping almost nineteen-fold since the end of 2022.

William Blair analysts led by Louie DiPalma reiterated their Underperform rating on the stock late Thursday. Palantir's U.S. government business experienced a pronounced revenue deceleration in 2021 and 2022 and DiPalma said "there is high risk of this happening again."

"A sharp revenue growth deceleration would likely result in valuation multiple contraction," he said, adding that William Blair sees greater than 20% downside to the shares over the next few years.

Barron's argued it may be time to sell Palantir stock earlier this week, before the slump, citing the rally and high valuation. The reasons behind that call are still in play.

Write to Callum Keown at callum.keown@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
